text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient - Routine Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Marguerite Okonkwo is a 49-year-old Nigerian-American female with a history of FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, c.5266dupC), presenting for her 24-month surveillance visit. She is currently on nira maintenance therapy and reports she is doing well.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo was initially diagnosed approximately 24 months ago with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO Stage IIIC (pT3cN1M0), presenting with massive ascites, peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, and bilateral pelvic/para-aortic lymphadenopathy. She had a markedly elevated CA-125 of 4,872 U/mL at diagnosis. She underwent primary debulking surgery achieving complete gross resection (R0), which included bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, appendectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, resection of sigmoid mesenteric implants, and right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping. Pathology confirmed high-grade serous carcinoma with serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) at the fimbriated ends of both fallopian tubes; 4 of 38 pelvic nodes and 2 of 22 para-aortic nodes were positive.

She subsequently completed 6 cycles of adjuvant Paraplatin (AUC 6) and Taxol (175 mg/m2) every 21 days. Her treatment course was notable for Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (stocking-and-glove distribution, managed without dose reduction), a Grade 2 carbo hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 4 (successfully re-challenged via a 12-step desensitization protocol), and Grade 2 alopecia. She achieved CA-125 normalization by end of treatment (CA-125 11 U/mL after cycle 6) with complete radiographic response on post-treatment CT imaging.

Given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and complete response to platinum-based therapy, she was started on Zejula maintenance at an individualized starting dose of 200 mg orally once daily (given weight <77 kg at 63 kg). Approximately 4 weeks into nira, she developed Grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets nadir 98,000/mcL), prompting a dose hold followed by a dose reduction to nira 100 mg orally once daily. She has remained on this reduced dose for approximately 18 months, tolerating it well. She was also found to have concurrent iron deficiency anemia (ferritin 12 ng/mL) attributed to surgical blood loss and chemotherapy-related marrow suppression, treated with oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg BID with subsequent hemoglobin recovery.

Today's visit represents her 24-month surveillance timepoint. Imaging was completed prior to today's visit. She reports feeling well overall. She has returned to her position as a high school principal, working full-time. She denies any symptoms that would raise concern for disease recurrence. Specifically, she denies new or progressive abdominal distension, bloating, early satiety, nausea, vomiting, change in bowel habits, hematochezia, melena, new or progressive pelvic pain, dysuria, urinary frequency, unexplained weight loss, new back pain, dyspnea, or hemoptysis. She does report ongoing vasomotor symptoms related to surgical menopause (hot flashes, night sweats), which she characterizes as manageable with her current non-hormonal regimen. She also notes mild vaginal dryness, well-controlled with vaginal moisturizer.

She brings up today that her 22-year-old daughter, who tested positive for the familial BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant at their last visit, has initiated enhanced surveillance (annual breast MRI and gynecologic surveillance). She is emotionally supportive of her daughter and finds comfort in the proactive approach. Her 19-year-old son tested negative for the variant. The patient has processed these results with the support of genetic counseling and the oncology social worker.

She raises a question about the possibility of discontinuing nira early, noting she has heard some patients stop after 2 years. She is well-informed, having read summaries of the PRIMA and PRIMA-Bin trial data.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue Grade 0-1 at most (minimal, not limiting activity). No fever, no chills, no night sweats other than menopausal. No significant unintentional weight change. Weight stable.
- HEENT: No headache, no visual changes, no epistaxis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema. Hypertension well-controlled on amlodipine.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or on exertion. No cough. No hemoptysis.
- GI: No abdominal pain. No nausea (resolved). No vomiting. No early satiety. No change in bowel habits. No rectal bleeding. No abdominal distension.
- GU/Gynecologic: No pelvic pain. No urinary symptoms. No vaginal bleeding (expected, status post hysterectomy and BSO). Mild vaginal dryness managed with moisturizer.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain. No new joint pain. Peripheral neuropathy essentially resolved - denies numbness or tingling in hands or feet at this time (Grade 0).
- Neurologic: No focal neurologic symptoms.
- Hematologic: No easy bruising, no petechiae, no unusual bleeding, no prolonged bleeding with minor cuts. No epistaxis.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine. Surgical menopause: hot flashes and night sweats present, managed with escitalopram.
- Psychiatric/Psychosocial: Good mood. Anxiety related to daughter's BRCA1 status acknowledged but managed. Continues to engage with oncology social work as needed. No depression.
- Skin: No rash. Hair has fully regrown (alopecia resolved).

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (for reference):
1. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO Stage IIIC - as detailed above
2. Mild hypothyroidism - on levothyroxine (well-controlled)
3. Hypertension - on amlodipine (well-controlled)
4. Surgical menopause (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy at time of primary debulking)
5. Osteopenia - T-score -1.2 at initial DXA, repeat T-score -1.4 at 24-month DXA (stable, no progression to osteoporosis)
6. Iron deficiency anemia - previously treated with oral iron supplementation; hemoglobin recovered
7. Status post total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, radical omentectomy, appendectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, sigmoid mesenteric implant resection, right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping

GERMLINE GENETICS:
- BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), Class 5 Pathogenic Variant - confirmed
- BRCA2 c.8167G>A (p.Asp2723Asn) - Variant of Uncertain Significance, not actionable

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: Ovarian cancer, diagnosed age 55, deceased
- Maternal aunt: Breast cancer, diagnosed age 42
- Daughter (age 22): BRCA1 c.5266dupC positive, enrolled in enhanced surveillance
- Son (age 19): BRCA1 negative

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Non-smoker, no significant alcohol use
- Works as a high school principal, returned to full-time employment
- G2P2, two adult children
- Lives with supportive spouse/partner
- Active in community; reports engaging in regular light exercise (walking 30 minutes most days)

ALLERGIES:
- Paraplatin: Hypersensitivity reaction (Grade 2 - flushing, urticaria, chest tightness); managed with desensitization protocol
- No other known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS:
1. Zejula (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime (reduced dose maintenance)
2. Ondansetron 8 mg orally 30 minutes prior to nira dose as needed for nausea (patient reports rarely using now)
3. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily on empty stomach
4. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
5. Escitalopram 10 mg orally once daily (non-hormonal management of vasomotor symptoms and mood support)
6. Calcium carbonate 1200 mg orally daily (in divided doses) with vitamin D 1000 IU daily (bone health given surgical menopause and osteopenia)
7. Vaginal moisturizer (Replens) as needed
8. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg - recently completed course; iron studies now normalized

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg (well-controlled), heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), SpO2 99% on room air. Weight: 64.2 kg. BMI: 22.8 kg/m2. ECOG Performance Status: 0.

General: Ms. Okonkwo appears well-nourished, in no acute distress, and in excellent overall health. Alert and oriented. Affect bright and engaged.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Hair has fully regrown.

Neck: Supple. No palpable thyroid nodule or goiter. No cervical lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, no wheezes, no dullness to percussion. No pleural rub.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed, no evidence of hernia along incision. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable masses. No fluid wave or shifting dullness to suggest ascites. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.

Pelvic/Genitourinary (limited external examination): Post-hysterectomy, post-BSO. No palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy. No groin masses. External genitalia unremarkable for examination performed.

Extremities: No edema. No petechiae or ecchymoses noted. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No calf tenderness.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral hands and feet - peripheral neuropathy has clinically resolved (Grade 0 at this visit). Coordination grossly intact. Gait normal.

Skin: No rash. No pallor clinically. Hair fully regrown.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, cervical, supraclavicular, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS:

Tumor Markers:
- CA-125: 12 U/mL (reference range <35 U/mL) - NORMAL, stable
- HE4: 52 pmol/L (reference range <140 pmol/L premenopausal, <150 pmol/L postmenopausal) - NORMAL

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.3 x10^9/L (reference 4.0-11.0)
- ANC: 3.1 x10^9/L (adequate)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (reference 12.0-16.0 g/dL; mild, stable, no symptoms)
- Hematocrit: 35.4%
- Platelets: 181,000/mcL (reference 150,000-400,000) - NORMAL, stable on Zejula 100 mg

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 103 mEq/L, CO2: 25 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.81 mg/dL (eGFR 76 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Glucose: 88 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.5 mg/dL, AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 18 U/L, alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L
- Total protein: 7.2 g/dL, Albumin: 4.1 g/dL

Iron Studies (checked today given prior iron deficiency):
- Ferritin: 28 ng/mL (recovered from prior nadir of 12 ng/mL) - lower end of normal, will monitor
- Serum iron: 78 mcg/dL, TIBC: 340 mcg/dL, transferrin saturation: 23% - adequate
- Ferrous sulfate course completed; iron stores have replenished

Thyroid:
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (reference 0.4-4.0) - NORMAL on levothyroxine, no dose adjustment needed

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed today):

24-Month Annual Surveillance CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast and CT Chest (performed prior to this visit):

The scan shows no evidence of disease. There are the expected post-surgical changes from prior cytoreductive surgery - the midline laparotomy scar tissue is noted, the uterus and bilateral adnexa are absent (status post hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy), and there is mild post-surgical adhesive-type change in the pelvis without bowel obstruction. The omentum is absent, replaced by expected post-omentectomy fatty streakiness in the upper abdomen. There is no ascites anywhere in the abdomen or pelvis. No peritoneal nodularity, no implants, no omental recurrence is seen. The liver is normal in size and density with no focal lesions. The spleen is normal. Kidneys are symmetric and unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the pelvis, retroperitoneum, or para-aortic distribution. No adnexal masses (expected given bilateral oophorectomy). The appendix is absent (post-appendectomy). CT chest shows no pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.

Overall, this is a clean scan - no findings to suggest recurrence at the 24-month mark.

DXA Bone Density (obtained as part of 24-month health maintenance, results reviewed):
- Lumbar spine (L1-L4) T-score: -1.4 (osteopenia range; -1.0 to -2.5)
- Left femoral neck T-score: -1.3 (osteopenia)
- Total hip T-score: -1.2
- Interpretation: Stable osteopenia, no progression to osteoporosis since prior scan (T-score -1.2 approximately 10 months ago). No vertebral fractures identified on lateral spine imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO STAGE IIIC, BRCA1-MUTATED - COMPLETE RESPONSE, ON Zejula MAINTENANCE

Ms. Okonkwo is now 24 months from her initial diagnosis and is in sustained complete remission. Her CA-125 remains comfortably within normal range at 12 U/mL, her HE4 is normal at 52 pmol/L, and her surveillance imaging is entirely free of disease. This is an encouraging milestone - she has now passed the 2-year mark following R0 primary debulking surgery and continues to do well on Zejula maintenance.

Regarding her question about early discontinuation of nira: I had an extensive discussion with her about the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial data and the PRIMA-Bin exploratory analyses. In the BRCA-mutated subgroup, Zejula maintenance demonstrated a substantial progression-free survival benefit compared to placebo, with the benefit appearing to extend with longer duration of maintenance in some analyses. The planned maintenance duration in the PRIMA trial was 36 months or until progression or unacceptable toxicity. Given that she is at the 18-month mark of Zejula (on track for the 36-month planned course), is tolerating the 100 mg dose well, has excellent hematologic stability, and has no new concerning toxicities, the risk-benefit ratio clearly favors continuing maintenance through the planned 36-month duration. I emphasized that premature discontinuation would deprive her of the remaining maintenance benefit that has been her best protection against microscopic residual disease. She appreciated this discussion and agreed to continue.

Plan:
- Continue nira (Zejula) 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime. Approximately 18 months remain in the planned 36-month maintenance course.
- CBC with differential every 4 weeks (ongoing hematologic monitoring; platelets and hemoglobin have been stable at reduced dose).
- CA-125 and HE4 every 3 months.
- Clinical examination every 3 months.
- Next annual surveillance CT in approximately 12 months (at 36-month timepoint), unless symptoms arise that warrant earlier imaging.
- If at any point CA-125 begins to rise from this nadir, or if clinical symptoms develop, imaging will be obtained promptly rather than waiting for the annual scan.

2. HEMATOLOGIC MONITORING ON Zejula

Hemoglobin is 11.8 g/dL - a mild, chronic, stable anemia consistent with the known myelosuppressive effects of Zejula. This has been stable and asymptomatic. Iron stores have replenished with her completed course of ferrous sulfate (ferritin now 28 ng/mL). Platelets at 181,000/mcL remain stable and well above the threshold of concern. No dose modification needed at this visit.

Plan: Continue monitoring CBC q4 weeks. Recheck ferritin in 3 months given it is at the lower end of normal. If hemoglobin were to fall below 8 g/dL or become symptomatic (Grade 3+), a dose hold/reduction would be considered; current Grade 1 level does not require intervention.

3. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE

Ms. Okonkwo underwent bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy at the time of her primary debulking surgery and has been in surgical menopause since. Given her BRCA1 status and ovarian cancer diagnosis, hormone replacement therapy is not recommended. She continues on escitalopram 10 mg daily for non-hormonal management of vasomotor symptoms, which she reports is providing meaningful benefit for both hot flashes and mood. Vaginal dryness is managed with Replens vaginal moisturizer.

Plan: Continue current regimen. If vaginal symptoms worsen, localized vaginal estrogen could be considered in limited amounts after a careful discussion of the risk-benefit in the context of her cancer history. Ongoing support from gynecology or menopause specialist available as needed.

4. OSTEOPENIA

Repeat DXA at 24 months shows T-scores ranging from -1.2 to -1.4 across measured sites - stable osteopenia with no progression to osteoporosis since her prior scan. This is expected in the context of surgical menopause and prior platinum-based chemotherapy. She is appropriately on calcium 1200 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU daily.

Plan: Continue calcium and vitamin D supplementation. No bisphosphonate indicated at this time given T-scores remain above -2.5 and no fragility fractures have occurred. Encourage weight-bearing exercise, which she is already doing (daily walking). Repeat DXA in 1-2 years.

5. HYPOTHYROIDISM

TSH 2.1 mIU/L - well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No dose adjustment needed.

Plan: Continue current dose. Recheck TSH annually.

6. HYPERTENSION

BP today 128/76 mmHg - well-controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily. Of note, she opted not to pursue Avastin as part of her treatment regimen given concern for hypertension exacerbation - a reasonable decision that proved moot given her complete response without it.

Plan: Continue amlodipine. Blood pressure monitoring at routine visits.

7. FAMILY/HEREDITARY CANCER IMPLICATIONS

Ms. Okonkwo's older child (age 22) has tested positive for the familial BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant and has been enrolled in enhanced surveillance at an appropriate facility (annual breast MRI, consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy around age 35-40 when childbearing is complete). Her younger child (age 19) tested negative - no additional cancer surveillance beyond population-based screening is needed for the son.

The patient was offered continued support through our genetic counseling team. She is coping with the news about her daughter with remarkable resilience and is channeling her experience into advocacy for her daughter's proactive health management.

Plan: Patient to continue receiving psychosocial support via oncology social work as desired. Genetic counseling team involved for ongoing family management.

8. QUALITY OF LIFE AND OVERALL FUNCTION

Ms. Okonkwo is performing at ECOG 0, working full-time as a school principal, exercising regularly, and reports a good quality of life. Peripheral neuropathy from her prior PTX exposure has clinically resolved to Grade 0. Alopecia has fully resolved. Nausea is no longer a significant issue. She is a remarkable example of successful recovery and maintenance.

Plan: Continue engagement with support services. Encourage ongoing physical activity. Nutritional counseling available if desired.

---

RETURN VISIT:
Patient to return in 3 months for routine surveillance (CA-125, HE4, CBC, clinical exam). Annual CT surveillance at approximately 36-month mark from diagnosis. She may reach out at any time if new symptoms develop.

Time spent with patient counseling and coordination of care: approximately 55 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gynecologic Oncology
